claim: Mixed-breed lost dogs traveled an average of 14 miles before pickup, compared to only 2 miles for purebred dogs — a 7x difference driven by rescuer perception of value.
stance_target: breed type as a predictor of expected travel distance for lost dogs
scope_conditions: Albrecht 1999 informal non-scientific study, n=254 dog owners; pre-social-media era (data collected 1999); not peer-reviewed
quoted_support: Mixed-breed dogs were allowed to travel an average of 14 miles before they were picked up, whereas purebred dogs were allowed to travel only an average of 2 miles before they were picked up.
numbers: 14 miles average travel for mixed-breed dogs
2 miles average travel for purebred dogs

claim: Toy breed dogs are the least likely to travel more than 0.75 miles from their last-seen point.
stance_target: toy breed dogs as a narrow-radius search case
scope_conditions: Albrecht 1999 informal study, n=254; not peer-reviewed
quoted_support: Toy breeds were the least likely to travel more than three-quarters of a mile from where they were last seen.

claim: Pointing breeds traveled farther than retrieving breeds when lost, suggesting hunting-drive intensity influences movement radius.
stance_target: breed-category behavioral priors for lost dog movement
scope_conditions: Albrecht 1999 informal study, n=254; single practitioner data collection
quoted_support: Breed and size seemed to have an effect on the distance traveled—pointing breeds were more likely to travel farther than retrieving breeds.

claim: Of 187 lost dogs in the Lord 2007 study, 71% were recovered — meaning 29% were never found, and the fate of this unrecovered cohort remains unresearched.
stance_target: significant non-recovery rate even in well-studied populations
scope_conditions: Lord et al. 2007, Dayton OH, 4-month study; data collected 2005
quoted_support: The study determined that of the 187 dogs that were lost, 132 (71%) of them were recovered. But when I read the study, my first question was this: What happened to the other 29% of lost dogs, and why were they never found?

claim: Fearful/undersocialized dogs are increasingly being transported cross-regionally for rehoming, and are at elevated escape risk during transport due to handler unpreparedness — a growing cause of lost-dog events.
stance_target: transport escape as an emerging high-risk lost-dog scenario
quoted_support: We have seen a dramatic increase in the number of escapes that come from these groups. Many undersocialized and skittish dogs that previously would not have been adopted from shelters are now being transported to be rehomed elsewhere in the country, and we suspect that some of these dogs are escaping due to various factors including carelessness and unpreparedness.

claim: Offering monetary rewards may counterproductively trigger chasing behavior by untrained rescuers, inadvertently driving fearful dogs into traffic; appeals to altruism may be more effective.
stance_target: reward framing as a behavioral variable in lost dog recovery
scope_conditions: Albrecht 2018 based on lost-pet Facebook group reports and neuroscience of reward vs. altruism centers (nucleus accumbens vs. posterior superior temporal sulcus)
quoted_support: Many well-meaning but untrained rescuers are inadvertently chasing skittish, loose dogs into traffic, with tragic consequences. Many volunteers with lost dog Facebook groups report that, in cases where a dog owner has offered a substantial reward, the lost dog was chased when sighted.

claim: Dogs that escape near isolated quiet areas (wooded areas, golf courses, cemeteries, large parks) tend to take up residence there, with fearful-temperament dogs preferring to remain in these low-stimulus zones.
stance_target: fear-driven spatial settling behavior in lost dogs
quoted_support: We've seen that many dogs with fearful temperaments that escape near isolated areas (wooded areas, golf courses, cemeteries, large parks, etc.) prefer to remain in those quiet areas, but we do not know what percentage.

claim: Geographic barriers — raging rivers, brick walls along freeways, canyons — are key direction-of-flight modulators for lost dogs that must be mapped as movement constraints in any search plan.
stance_target: terrain barrier integration into lost dog spatial probability model
quoted_support: A canyon, a brick wall along a freeway, and a raging river are all geographical barriers that would inhibit and influence a dog's direction of flight.
